message:
fix(daemon): eliminate connection cloning and handle requests concurrently

Client (daemon/client.rs):
- Replace the clone-based connection caching pattern with a mutex-guard
  approach via get_connection_locked(). Previously, the client would
  take() the stream from the mutex, clone it, put the clone back, and
  return the original. This was fragile: a failed clone would silently
  fall through to reconnect, and holding the original stream outside the
  lock created a window where two threads could use the same connection.
- Now the caller holds the MutexGuard for the duration of the request,
  ensuring exclusive access to the UnixStream. On I/O errors (write,
  read length, read payload), the connection is explicitly invalidated
  by setting *stream_guard = None before returning the error.
- The lock is dropped before decoding the response payload, so the
  connection is available to other threads while JSON deserialization
  runs (which can be non-trivial for large embedding responses).

Server (daemon/core.rs):
- Wrap the accept loop in std::thread::scope() and spawn each
  connection handler in a scoped thread. Previously, connections were
  handled serially on the accept thread, meaning a slow client blocked
  all other connections. Scoped threads ensure the daemon can serve
  multiple concurrent requests while still respecting the
  max_connections limit via the existing atomic counter.
- The connection counter decrement is now inside the spawned closure,
  correctly tracking active connections even when handlers run in
  parallel.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
